summary:
The Portuguese Empire (c. 1415–1999) was the first and longest-lived of the European overseas empires, pioneering the Age of Exploration under the guidance of [[event:3055|Henry the Navigator]] and establishing a global maritime trading network that stretched from Brazil to Africa, India, Southeast Asia, and East Asia — transforming world trade and permanently altering the trajectories of every society it touched. Portugal's small population (roughly 1 million in 1500) defied geographic logic in building an empire spanning three continents; the key was control of strategic chokepoints: Ceuta, Hormuz, Goa, Malacca — fortified trading posts (feitorias) rather than large territorial colonies. The [[event:3056|rounding of the Cape of Good Hope]] by Bartolomeu Dias (1488) and Vasco da Gama's voyage to India (1498) opened the direct sea route to the Asian spice trade, bypassing the Ottoman-controlled overland routes and giving Portugal a commercial monopoly that generated extraordinary wealth for decades.

Portugal's Estado da India (Indian Ocean State) was an aggressive maritime empire: the [[event:2488|Battle of Diu]] (1509) destroyed the combined Egyptian-Indian fleet and established Portuguese naval dominance in the Indian Ocean; the capture of [[event:2356|Goa]] (1510) and Malacca (1511) secured the two most critical trading nodes in Asian commerce. Portugal also claimed Brazil (1500) and established a [[event:2489|presence in Japan]], creating the global trading network that made Lisbon briefly the commercial capital of the world. The empire's weakness was demographic: Portugal lacked the population to garrison its vast network of fortresses, and the forced union with Spain (1580–1640) exposed Portuguese possessions to Spanish enemies — particularly the [[event:2326|Dutch]], who systematically dismantled Portuguese monopolies in Asia. By the 17th century, Portugal had ceded most of its Asian trade empire to the Dutch while retaining Brazil and African territories; the final remnant, Macau, was returned to China in 1999.
